content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:2b8f3739

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable facts about the user's homemade product business, dated market sales, upcoming event planning, product line, and chosen gift basket name/description, plus reusable rationale/context for autumn display and basket marketing.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I'm / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements and preferences should resolve to the stable USER entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=Harvest Festival Market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=OTHER; name=Harvest Festival Market; reason=Concrete reusable event mentioned as a planned participation target; no existing candidates.
- mention=Homemade and Handmade Market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=OTHER; name=Homemade and Handmade Market; reason=Concrete reusable event where the user sold products; no existing candidates.
- mention=homemade products; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's homemade products; reason=Recurring domain object for the user's products, but broad enough to canonicalize as the user's homemade products.
- mention=homemade jam; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's homemade jam; reason=Specific product repeatedly discussed and needed for later claims.
- mention=spice blends; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's spice blends; reason=Specific product repeatedly discussed and needed for later claims.
- mention=Harvest Gift Baskets; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PRODUCT; name=Harvest Gift Baskets; reason=Concrete product concept developed in the conversation before final naming.
- mention=Harvest Delight; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=Harvest Delight; reason=The user selected this as the name for the gift basket, making it a concrete reusable product identity.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|homemade products business|homemade jam|spice blends|Harvest Festival Market|Homemade and Handmade Market|Harvest Delight
        text_queries=Harvest Delight gift basket|Harvest Festival Market October 2 homemade jam spice blends|sold 15 jars homemade jam May 29 earning $225|autumn themed display homemade jam spice blends
        predicate_hints=has_goal|prefers|generated_artifact_detail|attended_event|current_metric_value

Session date: 2023/06/01 (Thu) 07:24
Transcript:
user: I'm considering participating in the Harvest Festival Market on October 2nd and I was wondering if you could help me come up with some ideas for eye-catching displays for my homemade products. By the way, I've had a pretty successful few weeks, I just sold 15 jars of my homemade jam at the Homemade and Handmade Market on May 29th, earning $225.
assistant: Congratulations on your success at the Homemade and Handmade Market! That's fantastic news!

Now, let's get creative and come up with some eye-catching display ideas for your homemade products at the Harvest Festival Market on October 2nd. Here are a few suggestions to get you started:

1. **Seasonal Theme**: Embrace the autumn theme and create a cozy, rustic display with warm colors, pumpkins, and fall leaves. Use burlap, twine, and natural elements to create a inviting atmosphere.
2. **Vintage Farmhouse**: Create a charming display that resembles a vintage farmhouse kitchen. Use old wooden crates, metal baskets, and distressed signs to showcase your homemade products.
3. **Product Pyramids**: Build pyramids using your jars of jam, honey, or other products to create a visually appealing display. You can also use baskets or containers to add texture and depth.
4. **Fresh Flower Arrangements**: Add a pop of color and freshness to your display with seasonal flowers or greenery. This will not only attract attention but also evoke a sense of wholesomeness and natural goodness.
5. **Tiered Displays**: Use tiered shelves or risers to create a multi-level display that showcases your products at different heights. This will add visual interest and make it easy for customers to browse your products.
6. **Baskets and Linens**: Use wicker baskets, linen cloths, and natural fibers to create a warm and inviting display. This will also help to evoke a sense of homemade goodness and craftsmanship.
7. **Signage and Storytelling**: Create a compelling story about your products and display process. Use chalkboard signs, wooden signs, or printed banners to highlight the quality, ingredients, and benefits of your homemade products.
8. **Themed Baskets**: Create themed baskets or gift sets that showcase your products in a unique and appealing way. For example, a "Harvest Delight" basket filled with your homemade jam, honey, and fresh bread.
9. **Lighting**: Use warm and soft lighting to create a cozy ambiance. String lights, lanterns, or candles can add a warm glow to your display and make it more inviting.
10. **Interactive Elements**: Encourage customers to engage with your display by adding interactive elements, such as a "Sample Station" or a "Guess the Flavor" game. This will help to break the ice and create a memorable experience.

Remember to keep your display easy to navigate, well-lit, and visually appealing. Make sure to have plenty of business cards and flyers on hand to promote your products and encourage customers to follow you on social media.

Good luck at the Harvest Festival Market, and I hope these ideas help you create an eye-catching display that showcases your amazing homemade products!
user: I like the idea of a seasonal theme, but I'm not sure how to incorporate it with my products. Can you give me some ideas on how to tie in my homemade jam and spice blends with the autumn theme?
assistant: Incorporating your products into the autumn theme can be done in a variety of ways. Here are some ideas to get you started:

**Homemade Jam:**

1. **Fall Fruit Feature**: Highlight the seasonal fruits used in your jam, such as apples, pears, or cranberries, by displaying them in a decorative bowl or basket near your jam jars. This will create a beautiful visual connection between the fruit and the finished product.
2. **Harvest-Inspired Labels**: Design special edition labels for your jam jars with autumn-themed graphics, such as leaves, acorns, or pumpkins. This will add a seasonal touch to your products.
3. **Apple Cider Pairing**: Offer a pairing suggestion by displaying your jam alongside a sign that says "Perfect with Apple Cider" or "Try with a Warm Cider Donut." This will evoke the cozy feelings of autumn.
4. **Fall Baking**: Create a display that showcases your jam as a key ingredient in autumn baking. Use a vintage baking stand or a wooden crate to display jars of jam, along with recipe cards for apple crisps, pear tarts, or other fall-themed treats.

**Spice Blends:**

1. **Pumpkin Spice Everything**: Create a "Pumpkin Spice Station" with your spice blend as the star. Offer samples of pumpkin spice-infused treats, such as cookies or lattes, and display them alongside your spice blend jars.
2. **Warm & Cozy**: Use warm lighting and cozy props like throw blankets, candles, or vintage lanterns to create an inviting atmosphere. This will help customers associate your spice blends with the comfort of autumn.
3. **Fall Baking Essentials**: Group your spice blends with other fall baking essentials like cinnamon sticks, nutmeg, and allspice to create a "Fall Baking Pantry" display.
4. **Autumnal Aromas**: Highlight the aromatic properties of your spice blends by creating a sensory experience. Use essential oils or room sprays with autumnal scents like cinnamon, clove, or pumpkin to create an immersive atmosphere.

**Combining Both Products:**

1. **Harvest Gift Baskets**: Create gift baskets or gift jars that combine your homemade jam and spice blends with other autumn-themed goodies like nuts, dried fruits, or tea.
2. **Fall Recipe Station**: Set up a recipe station with cards or a chalkboard that features recipes that incorporate both your jam and spice blends. This could include recipes for apple crisp, pumpkin pie, or spiced cider.
3. **Seasonal Flavors**: Emphasize the seasonal flavors of your products by creating a "Taste of Autumn" display. Use signs or banners to highlight the flavors and ingredients that are unique to the fall season.

These ideas should help you get started on creating a beautiful and cohesive autumn-themed display that showcases your homemade jam and spice blends. Good luck with your Harvest Festival Market!
user: I like the idea of the Harvest Gift Baskets. Can you give me some suggestions on what other items I could include in these baskets besides my jam and spice blends?
assistant: Harvest Gift Baskets are a wonderful way to create a unique and thoughtful offering for your customers. Here are some suggestions for other items you could include in your baskets:

**Food Items:**

1. **Artisanal Cheeses**: Partner with a local cheesemaker to include a wedge of artisanal cheese, such as a cheddar or gouda, that pairs well with your jam.
2. **Fresh Fruits**: Add some seasonal fruits like apples, pears, or persimmons to complement your jam and create a sweet and savory combination.
3. **Nuts**: Include a bag of roasted nuts like almonds, walnuts, or pecans to add crunch and texture to the basket.
4. **Dried Fruits**: Dried cranberries, apricots, or raisins can add natural sweetness and chewiness to the basket.
5. **Baked Goods**: Partner with a local baker to include a few sweet treats like cookies, muffins, or scones that incorporate your jam or spice blends.

**Beverages:**

1. **Specialty Teas**: Include a packet of specialty tea like apple cinnamon, pumpkin spice, or chai to create a cozy autumnal experience.
2. **Coffee or Hot Cocoa**: Add a bag of gourmet coffee or hot cocoa mix to complement your spice blends and create a warm, comforting drink.
3. **Cider or Apple Juice**: Partner with a local cider mill or juice producer to include a bottle of fresh cider or apple juice to pair with your jam.

**Home and Kitchen Items:**

1. **Candles or Soaps**: Include a seasonal-scented candle or a handmade soap in autumnal fragrances like pumpkin, apple, or spice to create a cozy atmosphere.
2. **Kitchen Linens**: Add a set of seasonal-themed kitchen linens like tea towels, aprons, or oven mitts to add a touch of autumn charm.
3. **Recipe Cards**: Create recipe cards with autumn-inspired recipes that incorporate your jam and spice blends, along with other seasonal ingredients.

**Other Ideas:**

1. **Pumpkin or Gourd**: Add a small, decorative pumpkin or gourd to create a festive touch.
2. **Fall-themed Decor**: Include a small, autumn-themed decoration like a leaf-shaped cookie cutter, a fall-colored potpourri, or a seasonal-themed coaster.
3. **Personalized Note**: Add a personalized note or card with a message, recipe, or tip to make the gift basket feel more special and thoughtful.

Remember to choose items that complement your products and fit your brand's aesthetic. You can also customize the baskets to fit different price points and themes. Good luck with your Harvest Gift Baskets!
user: Can you give me some ideas for a name for these gift baskets? I want something that captures the autumn theme and the idea of a thoughtful gift.
assistant: Here are some ideas for names for your autumn-themed gift baskets:

1. **Harvest Delight**: This name captures the essence of the autumn season and the delightful treats inside the basket.
2. **Cozy Autumn Nights**: This name evokes feelings of warmth and comfort, perfect for a gift basket that's meant to be enjoyed on a crisp autumn evening.
3. **Fall Favorites**: This name highlights the seasonal flavors and treats inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift.
4. **Autumn Harvest Basket**: This name is straightforward and informative, letting the recipient know exactly what to expect.
5. **Season's Bounty**: This name emphasizes the abundance of the harvest season and the thoughtful selection of items inside the basket.
6. **Warmth of the Harvest**: This name conveys the cozy, comforting feelings associated with autumn and the gift basket's contents.
7. **Gather 'Round**: This name has a friendly, inviting tone, suggesting a gift basket that's perfect for sharing with friends and family.
8. **Fall Flavors & Friends**: This name incorporates the idea of sharing the gift basket with others, while also highlighting the seasonal flavors inside.
9. **Harvest Home**: This name has a warm, welcoming tone, suggesting a gift basket that's perfect for enjoying at home with loved ones.
10. **Autumn Treasures**: This name emphasizes the special, treasured items inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift.
11. **Cozy Up**: This name is short and sweet, conveying the idea of a gift basket that's meant to be enjoyed on a cozy autumn day.
12. **Fall Fusion**: This name highlights the blend of flavors and items inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift that's a little bit different.

I hope one of these names sparks some inspiration for your gift baskets!
user: I think I'll go with "Harvest Delight". It's catchy and conveys the right message. Now, can you help me come up with a few sentences to describe the gift basket on my website or social media? I want to entice customers to buy it.
assistant: "Harvest Delight" is a wonderful name for your gift basket.

Here are a few options for describing your "Harvest Delight" gift basket on your website or social media:

**Option 1:**
Indulge in the flavors of the harvest season with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, filled with artisanal goodies like our signature homemade jam, warm spice blends, and crunchy nuts. Perfect for cozying up on a crisp autumn day!

**Option 2:**
Get ready to fall in love with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, carefully curated with a selection of autumnal treats that will warm your heart and home. From our homemade jam to specialty teas, every item is a delight for the senses!

**Option 3:**
Cozy up with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, the perfect way to celebrate the harvest season. Featuring our signature homemade jam, gourmet coffee, and other autumn-inspired goodies, this basket is sure to become your new favorite treat.

**Option 4:**
Welcome the harvest season with open arms and a full heart with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket. Filled with an assortment of artisanal treats, including our famous homemade jam and warm spice blends, this basket is the perfect way to share the joy of the season with friends and family.

Feel free to mix and match elements from each option to create the perfect description for your "Harvest Delight" gift basket. Good luck with your sales, and I hope your customers love your gift baskets!
user: I think I'll go with **Option 1**. It's short, catchy, and conveys the essence of the gift basket. Thank you so much for your help with the display ideas, product pairing suggestions, and gift basket name and description.
assistant: I'm so glad I could help! **Option 1** is a great choice - it's concise, inviting, and perfectly captures the essence of your "Harvest Delight" gift basket.

It was my pleasure to assist you with your display ideas, product pairing suggestions, and gift basket name and description. I hope your customers love your gift baskets and that they help make your Harvest Festival Market a huge success!

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, don't hesitate to ask. Otherwise, I wish you all the best with your market and your business. May your products bring joy and warmth to all who receive them!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

Platform: Mac OS X

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.

## Your Nature

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/06/01 (Thu) 07:24
Transcript:
user: I'm considering participating in the Harvest Festival Market on October 2nd and I was wondering if you could help me come up with some ideas for eye-catching displays for my homemade products. By the way, I've had a pretty successful few weeks, I just sold 15 jars of my homemade jam at the Homemade and Handmade Market on May 29th, earning $225.
assistant: Congratulations on your success at the Homemade and Handmade Market! That's fantastic news!

Now, let's get creative and come up with some eye-catching display ideas for your homemade products at the Harvest Festival Market on October 2nd. Here are a few suggestions to get you started:

1. **Seasonal Theme**: Embrace the autumn theme and create a cozy, rustic display with warm colors, pumpkins, and fall leaves. Use burlap, twine, and natural elements to create a inviting atmosphere.
2. **Vintage Farmhouse**: Create a charming display that resembles a vintage farmhouse kitchen. Use old wooden crates, metal baskets, and distressed signs to showcase your homemade products.
3. **Product Pyramids**: Build pyramids using your jars of jam, honey, or other products to create a visually appealing display. You can also use baskets or containers to add texture and depth.
4. **Fresh Flower Arrangements**: Add a pop of color and freshness to your display with seasonal flowers or greenery. This will not only attract attention but also evoke a sense of wholesomeness and natural goodness.
5. **Tiered Displays**: Use tiered shelves or risers to create a multi-level display that showcases your products at different heights. This will add visual interest and make it easy for customers to browse your products.
6. **Baskets and Linens**: Use wicker baskets, linen cloths, and natural fibers to create a warm and inviting display. This will also help to evoke a sense of homemade goodness and craftsmanship.
7. **Signage and Storytelling**: Create a compelling story about your products and display process. Use chalkboard signs, wooden signs, or printed banners to highlight the quality, ingredients, and benefits of your homemade products.
8. **Themed Baskets**: Create themed baskets or gift sets that showcase your products in a unique and appealing way. For example, a "Harvest Delight" basket filled with your homemade jam, honey, and fresh bread.
9. **Lighting**: Use warm and soft lighting to create a cozy ambiance. String lights, lanterns, or candles can add a warm glow to your display and make it more inviting.
10. **Interactive Elements**: Encourage customers to engage with your display by adding interactive elements, such as a "Sample Station" or a "Guess the Flavor" game. This will help to break the ice and create a memorable experience.

Remember to keep your display easy to navigate, well-lit, and visually appealing. Make sure to have plenty of business cards and flyers on hand to promote your products and encourage customers to follow you on social media.

Good luck at the Harvest Festival Market, and I hope these ideas help you create an eye-catching display that showcases your amazing homemade products!
user: I like the idea of a seasonal theme, but I'm not sure how to incorporate it with my products. Can you give me some ideas on how to tie in my homemade jam and spice blends with the autumn theme?
assistant: Incorporating your products into the autumn theme can be done in a variety of ways. Here are some ideas to get you started:

**Homemade Jam:**

1. **Fall Fruit Feature**: Highlight the seasonal fruits used in your jam, such as apples, pears, or cranberries, by displaying them in a decorative bowl or basket near your jam jars. This will create a beautiful visual connection between the fruit and the finished product.
2. **Harvest-Inspired Labels**: Design special edition labels for your jam jars with autumn-themed graphics, such as leaves, acorns, or pumpkins. This will add a seasonal touch to your products.
3. **Apple Cider Pairing**: Offer a pairing suggestion by displaying your jam alongside a sign that says "Perfect with Apple Cider" or "Try with a Warm Cider Donut." This will evoke the cozy feelings of autumn.
4. **Fall Baking**: Create a display that showcases your jam as a key ingredient in autumn baking. Use a vintage baking stand or a wooden crate to display jars of jam, along with recipe cards for apple crisps, pear tarts, or other fall-themed treats.

**Spice Blends:**

1. **Pumpkin Spice Everything**: Create a "Pumpkin Spice Station" with your spice blend as the star. Offer samples of pumpkin spice-infused treats, such as cookies or lattes, and display them alongside your spice blend jars.
2. **Warm & Cozy**: Use warm lighting and cozy props like throw blankets, candles, or vintage lanterns to create an inviting atmosphere. This will help customers associate your spice blends with the comfort of autumn.
3. **Fall Baking Essentials**: Group your spice blends with other fall baking essentials like cinnamon sticks, nutmeg, and allspice to create a "Fall Baking Pantry" display.
4. **Autumnal Aromas**: Highlight the aromatic properties of your spice blends by creating a sensory experience. Use essential oils or room sprays with autumnal scents like cinnamon, clove, or pumpkin to create an immersive atmosphere.

**Combining Both Products:**

1. **Harvest Gift Baskets**: Create gift baskets or gift jars that combine your homemade jam and spice blends with other autumn-themed goodies like nuts, dried fruits, or tea.
2. **Fall Recipe Station**: Set up a recipe station with cards or a chalkboard that features recipes that incorporate both your jam and spice blends. This could include recipes for apple crisp, pumpkin pie, or spiced cider.
3. **Seasonal Flavors**: Emphasize the seasonal flavors of your products by creating a "Taste of Autumn" display. Use signs or banners to highlight the flavors and ingredients that are unique to the fall season.

These ideas should help you get started on creating a beautiful and cohesive autumn-themed display that showcases your homemade jam and spice blends. Good luck with your Harvest Festival Market!
user: I like the idea of the Harvest Gift Baskets. Can you give me some suggestions on what other items I could include in these baskets besides my jam and spice blends?
assistant: Harvest Gift Baskets are a wonderful way to create a unique and thoughtful offering for your customers. Here are some suggestions for other items you could include in your baskets:

**Food Items:**

1. **Artisanal Cheeses**: Partner with a local cheesemaker to include a wedge of artisanal cheese, such as a cheddar or gouda, that pairs well with your jam.
2. **Fresh Fruits**: Add some seasonal fruits like apples, pears, or persimmons to complement your jam and create a sweet and savory combination.
3. **Nuts**: Include a bag of roasted nuts like almonds, walnuts, or pecans to add crunch and texture to the basket.
4. **Dried Fruits**: Dried cranberries, apricots, or raisins can add natural sweetness and chewiness to the basket.
5. **Baked Goods**: Partner with a local baker to include a few sweet treats like cookies, muffins, or scones that incorporate your jam or spice blends.

**Beverages:**

1. **Specialty Teas**: Include a packet of specialty tea like apple cinnamon, pumpkin spice, or chai to create a cozy autumnal experience.
2. **Coffee or Hot Cocoa**: Add a bag of gourmet coffee or hot cocoa mix to complement your spice blends and create a warm, comforting drink.
3. **Cider or Apple Juice**: Partner with a local cider mill or juice producer to include a bottle of fresh cider or apple juice to pair with your jam.

**Home and Kitchen Items:**

1. **Candles or Soaps**: Include a seasonal-scented candle or a handmade soap in autumnal fragrances like pumpkin, apple, or spice to create a cozy atmosphere.
2. **Kitchen Linens**: Add a set of seasonal-themed kitchen linens like tea towels, aprons, or oven mitts to add a touch of autumn charm.
3. **Recipe Cards**: Create recipe cards with autumn-inspired recipes that incorporate your jam and spice blends, along with other seasonal ingredients.

**Other Ideas:**

1. **Pumpkin or Gourd**: Add a small, decorative pumpkin or gourd to create a festive touch.
2. **Fall-themed Decor**: Include a small, autumn-themed decoration like a leaf-shaped cookie cutter, a fall-colored potpourri, or a seasonal-themed coaster.
3. **Personalized Note**: Add a personalized note or card with a message, recipe, or tip to make the gift basket feel more special and thoughtful.

Remember to choose items that complement your products and fit your brand's aesthetic. You can also customize the baskets to fit different price points and themes. Good luck with your Harvest Gift Baskets!
user: Can you give me some ideas for a name for these gift baskets? I want something that captures the autumn theme and the idea of a thoughtful gift.
assistant: Here are some ideas for names for your autumn-themed gift baskets:

1. **Harvest Delight**: This name captures the essence of the autumn season and the delightful treats inside the basket.
2. **Cozy Autumn Nights**: This name evokes feelings of warmth and comfort, perfect for a gift basket that's meant to be enjoyed on a crisp autumn evening.
3. **Fall Favorites**: This name highlights the seasonal flavors and treats inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift.
4. **Autumn Harvest Basket**: This name is straightforward and informative, letting the recipient know exactly what to expect.
5. **Season's Bounty**: This name emphasizes the abundance of the harvest season and the thoughtful selection of items inside the basket.
6. **Warmth of the Harvest**: This name conveys the cozy, comforting feelings associated with autumn and the gift basket's contents.
7. **Gather 'Round**: This name has a friendly, inviting tone, suggesting a gift basket that's perfect for sharing with friends and family.
8. **Fall Flavors & Friends**: This name incorporates the idea of sharing the gift basket with others, while also highlighting the seasonal flavors inside.
9. **Harvest Home**: This name has a warm, welcoming tone, suggesting a gift basket that's perfect for enjoying at home with loved ones.
10. **Autumn Treasures**: This name emphasizes the special, treasured items inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift.
11. **Cozy Up**: This name is short and sweet, conveying the idea of a gift basket that's meant to be enjoyed on a cozy autumn day.
12. **Fall Fusion**: This name highlights the blend of flavors and items inside the basket, making it a great choice for a gift that's a little bit different.

I hope one of these names sparks some inspiration for your gift baskets!
user: I think I'll go with "Harvest Delight". It's catchy and conveys the right message. Now, can you help me come up with a few sentences to describe the gift basket on my website or social media? I want to entice customers to buy it.
assistant: "Harvest Delight" is a wonderful name for your gift basket.

Here are a few options for describing your "Harvest Delight" gift basket on your website or social media:

**Option 1:**
Indulge in the flavors of the harvest season with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, filled with artisanal goodies like our signature homemade jam, warm spice blends, and crunchy nuts. Perfect for cozying up on a crisp autumn day!

**Option 2:**
Get ready to fall in love with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, carefully curated with a selection of autumnal treats that will warm your heart and home. From our homemade jam to specialty teas, every item is a delight for the senses!

**Option 3:**
Cozy up with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket, the perfect way to celebrate the harvest season. Featuring our signature homemade jam, gourmet coffee, and other autumn-inspired goodies, this basket is sure to become your new favorite treat.

**Option 4:**
Welcome the harvest season with open arms and a full heart with our "Harvest Delight" gift basket. Filled with an assortment of artisanal treats, including our famous homemade jam and warm spice blends, this basket is the perfect way to share the joy of the season with friends and family.

Feel free to mix and match elements from each option to create the perfect description for your "Harvest Delight" gift basket. Good luck with your sales, and I hope your customers love your gift baskets!
user: I think I'll go with **Option 1**. It's short, catchy, and conveys the essence of the gift basket. Thank you so much for your help with the display ideas, product pairing suggestions, and gift basket name and description.
assistant: I'm so glad I could help! **Option 1** is a great choice - it's concise, inviting, and perfectly captures the essence of your "Harvest Delight" gift basket.

It was my pleasure to assist you with your display ideas, product pairing suggestions, and gift basket name and description. I hope your customers love your gift baskets and that they help make your Harvest Festival Market a huge success!

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, don't hesitate to ask. Otherwise, I wish you all the best with your market and your business. May your products bring joy and warmth to all who receive them!
